cataloging_subject.label: The Proverbs of Hendyng: Gg.1.1 and Digby 86 versions
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The cataloged subject is Hermann Varnhagen's 1881 parallel publication of two complete manuscript versions of The Proverbs of Hendyng, from Cambridge University Library MS Gg.1.1 and Bodleian Library MS Digby 86, as represented in CME00091. The two encoded DIV1 elements and their 93 stanza groups are two transmission versions of one work, not 93 independent works. Varnhagen's German introduction, comparison table, and apparatus, the U-M encoding, the repository XML, and the physical witnesses remain distinct descriptive and lineage layers.

catalog_summary.title: The Proverbs of Hendyng
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous
catalog_summary.date: Thirteenth-century composition; earliest securely dated direct witness 1272–1282
catalog_summary.region: Medieval England; exact place of composition unknown

scope.description: Varnhagen's German heading for the edited texts

notes: The complete article title is Zu mittelenglischen Gedichten. XI. Zu den Sprichwörtern Hending's.

titles.value: Jesu Crist, al þis worldes red

scope.description: Normalized representative incipit for the Gg.1.1 and Digby 86 tradition; wording and spelling vary by witness

titles.value: Ici commence le livre de Hending

agents.name: Hendyng
notes: A traditional authority figure or persona within the poem; no historical author has been securely identified.

assertion.notes: Modern scholarship does not securely identify a historical author.
Hendyng functions as the recurring proverbial authority; the name may be a literary construction from hende rather than a recoverable historical author.
This is a broad scholarly date for the work, not a claim that either surviving version preserves an authorial original.

scope.description: First known full modern printing of the Harley 2253 version, which is related to but not one of CME00091's two complete version streams

assertion.notes: The work belongs to the Middle English literary tradition; its exact place of composition is unknown.

scope.description: One catalog hypothesis concerning production or textual affiliations of Gg.1.1

assertion.notes: This witness-level hypothesis must not be promoted to the work's place of composition.

scope.description: Recurring Quod Hending attribution formulae within otherwise Middle English stanzas

form_statements.description: Six-line aabccb stanza bodies, normally followed by a proverbial saying and the attribution formula Quod Hending; brief rubrics and formulae do not make the work mixed prose and verse.

summaries.value: The Proverbs of Hendyng is a thirteenth-century Middle English wisdom poem built from end-rhymed stanzas of counsel. A stanza develops advice about religious, domestic, economic, or social conduct and culminates in a compact proverbial saying attributed to Hendyng. The collection addresses practical Christian ethics for an adult audience that modern scholarship understands as potentially both monastic and lay. CME00091 presents Varnhagen's complete transcriptions of the Gg.1.1 and Digby 86 versions in sequence.

The first encoded division gives 46 stanzas from Cambridge University Library MS Gg.1.1; the second gives 47 stanzas from Bodleian Library MS Digby 86. Varnhagen's comparison reports 30 stanzas common to all three major versions, further partial overlap, and seven stanzas unique to each version. The two streams therefore transmit overlapping but materially different forms of one proverb work.

The record catalogs the two complete version streams as parts of an edited selection, not as independent works and not as 93 works. The prefixed Harley 2253 form indexed separately as DIMEV record 3383 / IMEV 2078 is represented as a related version. The tiny St John's F.8 fragments are supporting witnesses whose exact relationship remains incompletely described.

extent.description: Encoding metrics for the two version streams: 46 plus 47 stanza groups and 608 XML L elements. Of those L elements, 558 are the six-line stanza bodies and 50 are multi-line proverbial epigraphs; 70 additional single-line proverbial sayings are encoded as P rather than L. These are representation counts across two versions, not an intrinsic count of 93 separate works.

content_parts.label: Cambridge University Library MS Gg.1.1 version
content_parts.summary: Varnhagen's complete transcription of the 46-stanza Gg.1.1 version, beginning with an Old French rubric and transmitted at fols. 476v–479v.
notes: The first folio also contains preceding French text, and the final folio continues with a new Latin item; those neighboring texts are not part of Hendyng.
The 304 XML L elements comprise 276 stanza-body lines and 28 lines inside multi-line epigraphs.

content_parts.label: Bodleian Library MS Digby 86 version
content_parts.summary: Varnhagen's complete transcription of the 47-stanza Digby 86 version, under the rubric Hending þe hende and transmitted at fols. 140v–143r.
notes: On fol. 143r the poem ends in column a and Les proverbes del vilain begins in column b; the shared surface does not make the Old French work part of Hendyng.
The 304 XML L elements comprise 282 stanza-body lines and 22 lines inside multi-line epigraphs.

related_works.title: Mon that wol of wysdam heren (Harley 2253 prefixed version of The Proverbs of Hendyng)
notes: DIMEV indexes the prefixed Harley form separately from record 2800; this manifest does not silently merge the two records.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse, CME00091 XML source
evidence.summary: Establishes the encoded title, Varnhagen source citation, two-DIV1 structure, stanza sequence, rubrics, and encoded text.

evidence.citation: General Corpus structural count of CME00091.xml
evidence.summary: Counted 2 DIV1, 93 LG, 608 L, 70 P, and 94 EPIGRAPH/Q/BIBL structures; separated the two version streams and body-line versus epigraph-line metrics.

evidence.citation: Hermann Varnhagen, Zu mittelenglischen Gedichten. XI. Zu den Sprichwörtern Hending's, Anglia 4 (1881), pp. 180–200
evidence.locator: evidence:varnhagen-1881; cached complete volume in source-cache/CME00091/anglia-volume-4-1881-complete.pdf, PDF pp. 192–212
evidence.summary: Primary evidence for the immediate source edition, its complete Gg.1.1 and Digby 86 transcriptions, witness sigla, stanza comparison, and editorial conventions.

evidence.citation: Digital Index of Middle English Verse, version 1.0, records 2800 and 3383
evidence.locator: evidence:dimev-release; cached release source in source-cache/CME00091/dimev-v1.0.zip
evidence.summary: Supports the preferred title, incipit, six-line aabccb form, witness loci, separate indexing of the Harley prefixed form, and St John's fragments.

evidence.citation: Cambridge Digital Library, Cambridge University Library MS Gg.1.1
evidence.locator: evidence:cul-iiif and evidence:cul-visual; fols. 476v–479v, IIIF canvases 960–966
evidence.summary: Official catalog and complete facsimile evidence for date, material context, manuscript geography hypotheses, rubric, locus, and physical boundaries of the Gg.1.1 version.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries, MS Digby 86 catalog and Digital Bodleian complete facsimile
evidence.locator: evidence:bodleian-complete-iiif and evidence:bodleian-visual; fols. 140v–143r, IIIF canvases 288–293
evidence.summary: Official catalog and complete facsimile evidence for the 1272–1282 Worcestershire witness, rubric, locus, and shared-column ending boundary.

evidence.citation: Susan E. Deskis, Proverbs of Hendyng, Encyclopedia of Medieval Literature in Britain (2017), DOI 10.1002/9781118396957.wbemlb025
evidence.summary: Modern scholarly synthesis for the thirteenth-century date, wisdom-poem form, stanza-plus-proverb structure, audience, and three-version tradition.

evidence.citation: Cameron Louis, Authority in Middle English Proverb Literature, Florilegium 15 (1998), pp. 85–123, esp. 109–110
evidence.summary: Supports the analysis of Hendyng as a possibly constructed wise-man authority persona; its rhyme-scheme statement is not used because direct textual and DIMEV evidence supports aabccb.

evidence.citation: Thomas Wright and James Orchard Halliwell, Reliquiae Antiquae, vol. 1 (1845), pp. 109–116, 193–194, 256–257
evidence.summary: Supports the 1845 full Harley printing, short Gg.1.1 and Digby 86 excerpts, and the printed intermediary behind Varnhagen's H(alliwell) comparisons.

evidence.citation: General Corpus source-lineage manifest for CME00091
evidence.summary: Binds descriptive work metadata to the repository artifact, U-M encoding, scholarly editions, witnesses, facsimiles, catalog records, access paths, rights evidence, and unresolved questions.

open_questions.question: Is DIMEV record 3383's cross-reference to record 2834 an editorial error, and what relationship should the index formally assert between records 2800 and 3383?
next_steps: Ask the DIMEV editors whether record 3383 should cross-reference record 2800 rather than unrelated record 2834.
Retain separate related-work modeling until the index relationship is clarified.

open_questions.question: What are the present mounting, date, exact textual contents, and full photographic coverage of the Hendyng fragments in St John's College Cambridge MS F.8 (145)?
next_steps: Contact St John's Special Collections for the current catalog description and any preservation images.
Compare any supplied images against the Gg.1.1, Digby 86, and Harley version streams.

open_questions.question: Can the place or dialect region of the work's composition be distinguished from the production places and linguistic profiles of its surviving witnesses?
next_steps: Consult a current linguistic study of all three major versions rather than transferring any one witness's provenance to the work.

notes: The XML's 93 stanza groups are a serialization of two complete witness versions (46 plus 47), not a canonical 93-stanza work or a collection of 93 independent works.

Varnhagen directly prints Gg.1.1 and Digby 86 but identifies the Harley comparison readings as H(alliwell); the lineage therefore routes those comparisons through the 1845 Wright–Halliwell edition rather than claiming direct consultation of Harley 2253.
Modern German in Varnhagen's introduction and notes belongs to the scholarly edition layer, not the medieval work-language statement.

The complete CUL and Bodleian facsimiles are locally cached outside Git with exact IIIF links and work-canvas ranges in the lineage manifest; Bodleian's separate 23-image selection has no overlap with fols. 140v–143r.
